About Me

I am a driven Electrical Engineering graduate student at Brigham Young University, pursuing my Master's degree under the guidance of Professor Karl Warnick while maintaining a 3.85 GPA. My technical focus sits at the intersection of RF/microwave engineering, laser-based fabrication, and radar systems — a combination that lets me work on problems ranging from millimeter-wave radar to microscopic flexible interconnects.

My passion for hands-on engineering took root early — from winding the coils of a 500,000-Volt Tesla coil in 2022 to designing and building an 80 GHz radar system capable of detecting sub-millimeter high-velocity projectiles in 2025. Along the way, two summer internships at Raytheon's RF Products division in Tucson, AZ gave me industry-grade experience testing and simulating complex RF circuits for defense applications, validating my classroom intuitions in a professional lab environment.

Beyond the bench, I care deeply about mentorship and community. As a Teaching Assistant for Wireless Communication Circuits and EM Fields & Waves, I help the next generation of engineers build a rigorous foundation. I believe great engineering is as much about clear communication and collaborative problem-solving as it is about technical depth — a belief reinforced by two years of voluntary service and leadership roles in IEEE and Scouting.

Education

Anticipated Apr 2027 GPA 3.85

Master of Electrical Engineering

Brigham Young University — Provo, UT
Advisor: Prof. Karl Warnick

My graduate research sits within BYU's RF & Microwave group, focusing on simulation and fabrication of high-performance RF transmission structures using advanced EM solvers. I am developing methods for designing Coupled Line and Stepped Impedance bandpass filters and characterizing thin-film flex cable performance at RF frequencies — directly applicable to compact, lightweight defense and aerospace electronic systems.

Radar & Comm Systems Math of Signals & Systems Numerical Methods Applied EM & Optics Autonomous Aircraft Control
Graduated Apr 2025 GPA 3.98

B.S. Electrical Engineering — Magna Cum Laude

Brigham Young University — Provo, UT

Graduated near the top of my class with a near-perfect GPA, completing rigorous coursework spanning circuit analysis, electromagnetics, digital systems, and signal processing. My senior capstone project — an 80 GHz millimeter-wave radar for detecting high-speed projectiles — brought together every domain of my undergraduate training into a single working hardware system.

Work Experience

Masters Research Assistant

BYU — Electrical & Computer Engineering Sept 2025 – Present

As a graduate research assistant within BYU's RF/Microwave group, I investigate the electromagnetic behavior of ultra-thin flexible interconnects intended for next-generation compact RF systems. These structures must transmit signals with minimal loss and distortion even when bent or flexed — a critical requirement for wearable electronics and aerospace applications.

My day-to-day work involves constructing detailed 3-D EM models in HFSS and comparing simulated S-parameter responses against fabricated prototypes. I also design and simulate microwave bandpass filters — including Coupled Line and Stepped Impedance topologies — using Python-based synthesis scripts coupled to full-wave HFSS verification. The goal is to establish automated design workflows that can rapidly iterate on filter geometry for custom frequency bands.

HFSS Python Coupled Line Filters Stepped Impedance Filters S-Parameters RF Flex Cables

Teaching Assistant — Wireless Communication Circuits & EM Fields and Waves

BYU — Electrical & Computer Engineering Sept 2025 – Present

Supporting two courses simultaneously — Wireless Communication Circuits and EM Fields and Waves — I hold office hours, grade assignments, and run lab review sessions for undergraduate students navigating some of the most mathematically demanding material in the EE curriculum.

  • Tutored students on Stub and Lumped Element Matching Networks, walking through Smith Chart derivations and verifying solutions with ADS simulations.
  • Explained Noise Figure, Gain Circles, and Stability Circles in amplifier design, translating abstract stability theory into concrete CAD workflows.
  • Developed example problems and supplementary notes that clarified common misconceptions around transmission-line theory.
Impedance Matching Smith Chart Noise Analysis Stability Theory ADS

RF Products Summer Intern

Raytheon — Tucson, AZ May–Aug 2024 & May–Aug 2025

Raytheon's RF Products division designs and manufactures precision radar and electronic warfare systems for the U.S. Department of Defense. Over two consecutive summers, I embedded in a hardware team responsible for RF exciter and receiver circuit cards — systems that must perform reliably across extreme temperature ranges and dense electromagnetic environments.

My primary contributions centered on closing the loop between simulation and hardware. I built Python test scripts that automated multi-point S-parameter and power measurements, reducing measurement time and eliminating manual transcription errors. Using SystemVue and ADS, I modeled entire signal chain architectures and ran Monte Carlo sweeps over temperature and component tolerance to predict hardware performance before physical builds. When actual hardware arrived, I correlated measured data against simulation to validate component substitutions and recommend design changes.

  • Tested and troubleshot RF circuit cards using Network Analyzers, Spectrum Analyzers, and Signal Generators, diagnosing component-level failures.
  • Assisted with EME/EMI (Electromagnetic Effects / Electromagnetic Interference) testing, ensuring circuit cards met military radiated emission standards.
  • Demonstrated that simulation-to-hardware correlation within ±1 dB is achievable with careful material modeling and de-embedding techniques.
SystemVue ADS Python Network Analyzer Spectrum Analyzer Signal Generator EME/EMI RF Circuit Cards

Research Assistant — Laser Ablation Lab

BYU — Electrical & Computer Engineering May 2023 – May 2024 & Sept 2024 – Apr 2025

The BYU Laser Ablation Lab explores direct-write laser processing as a pathway to fabricating precision RF structures without the waste and cost of traditional photolithography. I joined the group to develop a laser ablation workflow capable of producing two critical RF components: metal mesh bandpass filters for optical and RF frequencies, and microscopic flexible cables for compact interconnect applications.

Laser ablation requires balancing pulse energy, repetition rate, scan speed, and focus position to achieve clean material removal with micron-level dimensional accuracy. I characterized these process parameters systematically, then translated the optimized process into a repeatable fabrication protocol. This work culminated in an IEEE conference publication — my first peer-reviewed paper — presented at the 2024 Intermountain Engineering, Technology and Computing (IETC) conference.

Laser Ablation Direct-Write Fabrication RF Metal Mesh Filters Flex Cable Fabrication Process Characterization

Teaching Assistant — Circuit Analysis

BYU — Electrical & Computer Engineering Sept 2023 – Dec 2023

Provided tutoring and hands-on lab support for students learning foundational circuit analysis — KVL, KCL, Thévenin/Norton equivalents, transient response, and frequency-domain analysis. Helped students bridge the gap between pen-and-paper theory and physical breadboard circuits.

Circuit Analysis LTSpice Lab Instruction

Research Assistant — Photonic Components

BYU — Electrical & Computer Engineering Sept 2022 – May 2023

My first research role at BYU introduced me to the simulation of photonic integrated circuit (PIC) components. Using Python, I modeled waveguide structures, ring resonators, and couplers, extracting S-parameters to predict insertion loss and cross-talk. This experience laid the foundation for the RF simulation work I do today — the mathematics of scattering parameters is universal across microwave and optical frequencies.

Python Photonic Simulation S-Parameters Waveguide Modeling

Selected Projects

📡 *** 1 — 80 GHz mmWave radar hardware setup in laboratory

Senior Capstone · 2024–2025

80 GHz mmWave Radar — Projectile Detection

Small, high-velocity projectiles such as pellets or fragments are notoriously difficult to track optically in a lab environment. Our senior capstone team tackled this by designing a complete 80 GHz frequency-modulated continuous-wave (FMCW) radar system from antenna to digital back-end — entirely in-house at BYU.

My role: I led the RF front-end design, contributing to antenna selection, transmit/receive circuit layout, and signal conditioning. I also handled the Python-based signal processing pipeline that extracted range and velocity from raw ADC data using FFT techniques.

  • Full FMCW radar architecture operating at 80 GHz (W-band)
  • Custom PCB RF front-end with matched antenna elements
  • Python DSP pipeline: windowed FFT → range-Doppler map
  • Demonstrated detection of sub-centimeter high-velocity objects
  • Validated against known range targets before live projectile tests
80 GHz FMCW Python DSP KiCad PCB HFSS Range-Doppler
🔬 *** 2 — Microscope image of laser-ablated high-density flex cable traces

Research Project · 2023–2025

High-Density Flex Cables via Direct-Write Laser Ablation

Traditional photolithographic flex cable fabrication is expensive and slow for small-batch research prototyping. This project developed a direct-write laser ablation process to pattern micron-scale metal traces on flexible polymer substrates — enabling rapid iteration without masks or chemical etchants.

The process was optimized by sweeping laser pulse energy, scan speed, and focus offset, then characterizing trace geometry under optical microscopy and measuring RF transmission with a network analyzer. The work resulted in a peer-reviewed IEEE publication.

  • Achieved trace widths below 50 µm on flexible polyimide substrates
  • Characterized RF insertion loss vs. trace geometry across frequency
  • Established repeatable process recipe for in-house rapid prototyping
  • Published and presented at 2024 IEEE IETC conference
Laser Ablation Flex Substrate Network Analyzer Optical Microscopy Process Dev.
🎯 *** 3 — Laser tag game system PCB prototype and assembled unit

Junior Team Design · 2023–2024

Laser Tag Game System & IR "Grenade"

Working as part of a student engineering team, we built a complete laser tag game system from scratch — including handheld tagger units, wearable hit sensors, and an area-effect IR "grenade" that simulates an explosion by broadcasting in all directions simultaneously.

My contributions: I designed the IR transmit and receive circuit boards in KiCad, specified and sourced components, wrote the embedded game state machine in C, and implemented the hardware drivers for the IR emitter arrays on the grenade unit.

  • Modulated IR transmitter circuit with 38 kHz carrier encoding
  • Wearable multi-direction IR receiver vest with hit detection logic
  • IR "grenade" with 360° omni-directional emitter array
  • C game engine: lives, scoring, team modes on embedded MCU
  • Custom PCB designed in KiCad and manufactured at low cost
KiCad PCB Embedded C IR Circuits Power Systems Game Logic
⚡ *** 4 — 500,000-Volt Tesla coil producing high-voltage electrical arcs during demonstration

Personal Engineering Project · 2022

500,000-Volt Solid-State Tesla Coil

Motivated by a fascination with high-voltage resonant systems, I independently designed, built, and safely demonstrated a solid-state Tesla coil producing arcs at 500,000 volts — one of the most dramatically visual expressions of electromagnetic resonance an engineer can build.

The project demanded mastery of resonant circuit design, high-voltage insulation, power electronics, and safety engineering simultaneously. I modeled the resonant frequency of the secondary coil, designed the primary driver circuit, wound both coils by hand, and engineered a safety perimeter system before public demonstrations.

  • Solid-state SSTC design with IGBT full-bridge driver
  • Resonant secondary wound to achieve 500 kV output
  • Designed high-voltage insulation and grounding scheme
  • Successfully demonstrated safely to multiple live audiences
High-Voltage Design Resonant Circuits Power Electronics Safety Engineering

Publications

"Fabrication of High Density Flex Cables Using Direct Write Laser Ablation"

J. Kartchner, B. Ferguson, D. Jensen, J. Payne, G. Nielson and S. Schultz
2024 Intermountain Engineering, Technology and Computing (IETC) — Logan, UT, USA, 2024, pp. 135–140

This paper presents a direct-write laser ablation technique for patterning high-density metallic traces on flexible substrates, enabling rapid prototyping of RF flex cable interconnects without photolithographic masks. We demonstrate sub-50 µm trace widths and characterize RF transmission performance across frequency, validating the process as a viable path to compact, low-loss flexible interconnects for defense and consumer electronics.

View on IEEE Xplore — DOI: 10.1109/IETC61393.2024.10564256

Engineering Approach

Whether I am designing a microwave filter, characterizing a fabrication process, or debugging a radar signal chain, I follow a consistent methodology that connects theory to hardware and simulation to measurement:

1

Define

Clarify specifications: frequency, loss, power, size, temperature range. A precise spec drives every decision downstream.

2

Model

Build analytical or circuit-level models first (Python/MATLAB/ADS) to understand design space before committing to full-wave EM.

3

Simulate

Validate with 3-D EM (HFSS) or system-level simulation (SystemVue). Sweep parameters and corner cases before taping out.

4

Build & Measure

Fabricate or assemble hardware; measure with calibrated instruments. Document every deviation from the expected result.

5

Correlate

Overlay simulation on measurement. Discrepancies are the learning. Update models, iterate, and document the updated understanding.

Community & Leadership

IEEE — BYU Chapter Secretary

Served as Secretary for the BYU chapter of IEEE during the 2023–2024 academic year. Helped organize and run professional development events, guest lectures, and networking mixers that connected students with practicing engineers across Utah's tech and defense industry.

Voluntary Mission (2 Years)

Completed a two-year voluntary mission for The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. Living and serving full-time in a Portuguese-speaking community built language fluency, cross-cultural communication skills, and deep interpersonal resilience — skills that directly translate to collaborative engineering and leadership environments.

Teaching Assistant (Multiple Terms)

Across four semesters as a TA (Circuit Analysis, EM Fields, Wireless Comm Circuits), I helped hundreds of undergraduate students master challenging EE material. Teaching reinforces my own understanding and reflects a commitment to giving back to the academic community that trained me.

Eagle Scout — 2016

Earning the Eagle Scout rank required years of consistent effort, leadership of a community service project, and demonstrated character. The skills learned — planning under constraint, leading peers, and following through on long-horizon goals — remain foundational to my work as an engineer and researcher.
